Mutli Model 1

Model 1: Binary Admission Severity Classification

This model predicts COVID-19 severity at admission using clinical and metabolomic features. The binary target classes are non_severe and severe, where moderate patients are treated as non_severe, and severe/critical patients are combined as severe.

library(reticulate)

py_require(c(
  "pandas",
  "numpy",
  "matplotlib",
  "seaborn",
  "scikit-learn",
  "xgboost",
  "joblib"
))
# ============================================================
# SETUP
# ============================================================

import os
import re
import hashlib
import joblib
import warnings

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

from sklearn.model_selection import GroupKFold, GridSearchCV, cross_validate, cross_val_predict
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, LabelEncoder
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import ExtraTreesClassifier
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from xgboost import XGBClassifier

from sklearn.metrics import (
    classification_report,
    ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
    roc_auc_score,
    average_precision_score,
    f1_score,
    balanced_accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score
)

RANDOM_STATE = 42

CACHE_VERSION = "model_1"
CACHE_DIR = "cached_model_1"
os.makedirs(CACHE_DIR, exist_ok=True)

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")

Load Data

# ============================================================
# LOAD DATA
# ============================================================

# Change this path if needed.
df = pd.read_csv("model1dataset.csv")
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

print("Raw data shape:", df.shape)

Create Severity Target

# ============================================================
# CREATE MODEL 1 SEVERITY TARGET
# ============================================================

TARGET_COL_RAW = "Severity on admission (Moderate, severe, critical)"
GROUP_COL_RAW = "Sample ID"

# Clean common missing value markers.
df = df.replace(["-", "None", "", " ", "NA", "N/A", "nan", "NaN"], np.nan)

# Binary severity target:
# moderate = non_severe
# severe + critical = severe

df["severity_class"] = (
    df[TARGET_COL_RAW]
    .astype(str)
    .str.strip()
    .str.lower()
    .replace({
        "moderate": "non_severe",
        "severe": "severe",
        "critical": "severe"
    })
)

valid_classes = ["non_severe", "severe"]

# Keep only valid binary severity labels.
df_model = df[df["severity_class"].isin(valid_classes)].copy()

# Make a group ID. This dataset appears to have one row per patient/sample, so Sample ID is used.
# If Sample ID is missing, use row number to avoid breaking GroupKFold.
df_model["patient_group"] = df_model[GROUP_COL_RAW].astype(str)
df_model.loc[df_model[GROUP_COL_RAW].isna(), "patient_group"] = "row_" + df_model.index.astype(str)

print("Severity target counts:")
Severity target counts:
print(df_model["severity_class"].value_counts())
severity_class
severe        25
non_severe    14
Name: count, dtype: int64
print("\nUnique patient/sample groups:")

Unique patient/sample groups:
print(df_model["patient_group"].nunique())
39

Define Candidate Predictors

# ============================================================
# DEFINE CANDIDATE PREDICTORS
# ============================================================

# Columns that should NOT be used as predictors because they are identifiers, labels,
# or outcome variables that may leak severity information.
exclude_cols = {
    GROUP_COL_RAW,
    TARGET_COL_RAW,
    "severity_class",
    "patient_group",
    "Survived",
    "Intubated",
    "Pneumonia diagnosis",
    "Kidney injury",
    "Liver injury",
    "Vascular injury",
    "CNS injury"
}

# Use all remaining admission clinical + metabolomic variables as candidate predictors.
# This keeps the modelling approach aligned with Model 2 while adapting preprocessing to this dataset.
feature_cols_raw = [c for c in df_model.columns if c not in exclude_cols]

print("Initial candidate predictors:", len(feature_cols_raw))
Initial candidate predictors: 578
print("Excluded columns:", sorted([c for c in exclude_cols if c in df_model.columns]))
Excluded columns: ['CNS injury', 'Intubated', 'Kidney injury', 'Liver injury', 'Pneumonia diagnosis', 'Sample ID', 'Severity on admission (Moderate, severe, critical)', 'Survived', 'Vascular injury', 'patient_group', 'severity_class']

Clean Model Dataset

# ============================================================
# CLEAN MODEL DATA
# ============================================================

model_df = df_model[["patient_group", "severity_class"] + feature_cols_raw].copy()

# Helper function to convert messy numeric columns such as '< LOD' and '0.33 (ug/mL)'.
def extract_numeric_series(series):
    cleaned = series.astype(str).str.strip()
    cleaned = cleaned.replace({
        "< LOD": np.nan,
        "<LOD": np.nan,
        "LOD": np.nan,
        "nan": np.nan,
        "None": np.nan,
        "-": np.nan,
        "": np.nan
    })
    extracted = cleaned.str.extract(r"([-+]?\d*\.?\d+(?:[eE][-+]?\d+)?)", expand=False)
    return pd.to_numeric(extracted, errors="coerce")

# Convert numeric-looking object columns to numeric.
# Truly categorical columns, such as Sex, are one-hot encoded.
feature_cols_current = [c for c in model_df.columns if c not in ["patient_group", "severity_class"]]
categorical_cols = []

for col in feature_cols_current:
    if model_df[col].dtype == "object":
        numeric_version = extract_numeric_series(model_df[col])
        non_missing_original = model_df[col].notna().sum()
        numeric_success = numeric_version.notna().sum()
        success_ratio = numeric_success / max(non_missing_original, 1)

        if success_ratio >= 0.50:
            model_df[col] = numeric_version
        else:
            categorical_cols.append(col)
    else:
        model_df[col] = pd.to_numeric(model_df[col], errors="coerce")

print("Categorical columns one-hot encoded:", categorical_cols)
Categorical columns one-hot encoded: []
if len(categorical_cols) > 0:
    model_df = pd.get_dummies(
        model_df,
        columns=categorical_cols,
        drop_first=True,
        dummy_na=False
    )

feature_cols = [c for c in model_df.columns if c not in ["patient_group", "severity_class"]]

# Remove features with too much missingness. Proposal used >30%; adjust if needed.
MISSING_THRESHOLD = 0.30
missing_rate = model_df[feature_cols].isna().mean()
drop_missing = missing_rate[missing_rate > MISSING_THRESHOLD].index.tolist()
model_df = model_df.drop(columns=drop_missing)

feature_cols = [c for c in model_df.columns if c not in ["patient_group", "severity_class"]]

# Remove constant columns.
constant_cols = [
    c for c in feature_cols
    if model_df[c].nunique(dropna=True) <= 1
]
model_df = model_df.drop(columns=constant_cols)

feature_cols = [c for c in model_df.columns if c not in ["patient_group", "severity_class"]]

print("Final model dataframe shape:", model_df.shape)
Final model dataframe shape: (39, 570)
print("Dropped high-missing features:", len(drop_missing))
Dropped high-missing features: 8
print("Dropped constant features:", len(constant_cols))
Dropped constant features: 2
print("\nFinal class counts:")

Final class counts:
print(model_df["severity_class"].value_counts())
severity_class
severe        25
non_severe    14
Name: count, dtype: int64
print("\nFinal unique groups:")

Final unique groups:
print(model_df["patient_group"].nunique())
39
print("\nFinal predictors:", len(feature_cols))

Final predictors: 568

Prepare Modelling Objects

# ============================================================
# PREPARE X, y, GROUPS
# ============================================================

X = model_df.drop(columns=["patient_group", "severity_class"])
X.columns = X.columns.astype(str)

# Fixed binary class order for interpretability.
target_names = ["non_severe", "severe"]

label_encoder = LabelEncoder()
label_encoder.fit(target_names)
LabelEncoder()
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters 
y_raw = model_df["severity_class"].astype(str)
y = label_encoder.transform(y_raw)
groups = model_df["patient_group"].astype(str)

print("Class mapping:")
Class mapping:
for cls, encoded in zip(label_encoder.classes_, label_encoder.transform(label_encoder.classes_)):
    print(f"{cls} -> {encoded}")
non_severe -> 0
severe -> 1
print("\nSamples:", X.shape[0])

Samples: 39
print("Predictors:", X.shape[1])
Predictors: 568
print("Unique patient/sample groups:", groups.nunique())
Unique patient/sample groups: 39
print("\nClass proportions:")

Class proportions:
print(y_raw.value_counts(normalize=True).reindex(target_names))
severity_class
non_severe    0.358974
severe        0.641026
Name: proportion, dtype: float64

n_classes = len(label_encoder.classes_)
severe_class = label_encoder.transform(["severe"])[0]

Class Distribution Plot

# ============================================================
# CLASS DISTRIBUTION PLOT
# ============================================================

class_counts = y_raw.value_counts().reindex(target_names)

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.barplot(x=class_counts.index, y=class_counts.values)

plt.title("Model 1 Binary Admission Severity Class Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Severity Class")
plt.ylabel("Number of Samples")

for i, value in enumerate(class_counts.values):
    plt.text(i, value + 0.5, str(value), ha="center")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Define Cache Key

# ============================================================
# CACHE KEY
# ============================================================

cache_string = (
    CACHE_VERSION
    + str(X.shape)
    + str(list(X.columns))
    + str(pd.Series(y).value_counts().to_dict())
)

cache_key = hashlib.md5(cache_string.encode()).hexdigest()[:10]

print("Cache key:", cache_key)
Cache key: 940281cf8e

Feature Selection Object

# ============================================================
# FEATURE SELECTION OBJECT
# ============================================================

# Same feature selection logic as Model 2:
# Extra Trees ranks predictors, SelectFromModel keeps the top selected features.
# This happens inside the CV pipeline to avoid feature-selection leakage.

max_selected_features = min(100, X.shape[1])

et_selector = SelectFromModel(
    ExtraTreesClassifier(
        n_estimators=250,
        class_weight="balanced",
        random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
        max_features="sqrt",
        min_samples_leaf=2,
        n_jobs=1
    ),
    max_features=max_selected_features
)

Define Six Models

# ============================================================
# SIX MODEL DEFINITIONS + HYPERPARAMETER GRIDS
# ============================================================

# Same model families and tuning logic as Model 2, adapted for binary severity prediction.

model_specs = {

    "Extra Trees": {
        "pipeline": Pipeline([
            ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
            ("feature_selection", et_selector),
            ("model", ExtraTreesClassifier(
                class_weight="balanced",
                random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
                n_jobs=1
            ))
        ]),
        "param_grid": {
            "feature_selection__max_features":[5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, 100],
            "model__n_estimators": [250, 400],
            "model__max_features": ["sqrt", 0.3],
            "model__min_samples_leaf": [2, 3],
            "model__max_depth": [None, 8]
        }
    },

    "XGBoost": {
        "pipeline": Pipeline([
            ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
            ("feature_selection", et_selector),
            ("model", XGBClassifier(
                objective="binary:logistic",
                eval_metric="logloss",
                random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
                n_jobs=1
            ))
        ]),
        "param_grid": {
            "feature_selection__max_features": [5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, 100],
            "model__n_estimators": [150, 250],
            "model__max_depth": [2, 3],
            "model__learning_rate": [0.03, 0.05],
            "model__subsample": [0.8, 1.0],
            "model__colsample_bytree": [0.8, 1.0]
        }
    },

    "Elastic Net Logistic Regression": {
        "pipeline": Pipeline([
            ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
            ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
            ("feature_selection", et_selector),
            ("model", LogisticRegression(
                max_iter=5000,
                class_weight="balanced",
                penalty="elasticnet",
                solver="saga",
                random_state=RANDOM_STATE
            ))
        ]),
        "param_grid": {
            "feature_selection__max_features":[5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, 100],
            "model__C": [0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0],
            "model__l1_ratio": [0.25, 0.5, 0.75]
        }
    },

    "SVM RBF": {
        "pipeline": Pipeline([
            ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
            ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
            ("feature_selection", et_selector),
            ("model", SVC(
                kernel="rbf",
                probability=True,
                class_weight="balanced",
                random_state=RANDOM_STATE
            ))
        ]),
        "param_grid": {
            "feature_selection__max_features": [5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, 100],
            "model__C": [0.5, 1.0, 2.0],
            "model__gamma": ["scale", 0.01]
        }
    },

    "Gaussian Naive Bayes": {
        "pipeline": Pipeline([
            ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
            ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
            ("feature_selection", et_selector),
            ("model", GaussianNB())
        ]),
        "param_grid": {
            "feature_selection__max_features": [5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, 100],
            "model__var_smoothing": [1e-9, 1e-8, 1e-7, 1e-6]
        }
    },

    "MLP Neural Network": {
        "pipeline": Pipeline([
            ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
            ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
            ("feature_selection", et_selector),
            ("model", MLPClassifier(
                activation="relu",
                solver="adam",
                early_stopping=True,
                validation_fraction=0.2,
                n_iter_no_change=30,
                max_iter=1500,
                random_state=RANDOM_STATE
            ))
        ]),
        "param_grid": {
            "feature_selection__max_features":[5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 75, 100],
            "model__hidden_layer_sizes": [
                (16,),
                (32,),
                (32, 16),
                (64, 32),
                (64, 32, 16)
            ],
            "model__alpha": [0.001, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1],
            "model__learning_rate_init": [0.001, 0.0005, 0.0001],
            "model__batch_size": [16, 32, 64]
        }
    }
}

print("Models included:")
Models included:
for model_name in model_specs.keys():
    print("-", model_name)
- Extra Trees
- XGBoost
- Elastic Net Logistic Regression
- SVM RBF
- Gaussian Naive Bayes
- MLP Neural Network

Cached Hyperparameter Tuning

# ============================================================
# CACHED GROUPED HYPERPARAMETER TUNING
# ============================================================

# Same optimisation metric as Model 2: Macro F1.
# This is appropriate for binary severity classification because both classes matter.

inner_cv = GroupKFold(n_splits=5)

tuned_results = {}
best_estimators = {}

for name, spec in model_specs.items():

    safe_name = name.replace(" ", "_").replace("/", "_")
    cache_path = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, f"{cache_key}_{safe_name}_best_model.joblib")

    if os.path.exists(cache_path):
        print(f"\nLoading cached tuned model: {name}")

        cached = joblib.load(cache_path)

        best_estimators[name] = cached["best_estimator"]
        tuned_results[name] = cached["tuned_result"]

        continue

    print(f"\nTuning model: {name}")

    search = GridSearchCV(
        estimator=spec["pipeline"],
        param_grid=spec["param_grid"],
        scoring="f1_macro",
        cv=inner_cv,
        n_jobs=1,
        error_score="raise",
        refit=True
    )

    search.fit(X, y, groups=groups)

    tuned_results[name] = {
        "Best CV Macro F1": search.best_score_,
        "Best Params": search.best_params_
    }

    best_estimators[name] = search.best_estimator_

    joblib.dump({
        "best_estimator": search.best_estimator_,
        "tuned_result": tuned_results[name]
    }, cache_path)

    print("Best CV Macro F1:", round(search.best_score_, 3))
    print("Saved cache:", cache_path)

Loading cached tuned model: Extra Trees

Loading cached tuned model: XGBoost

Loading cached tuned model: Elastic Net Logistic Regression

Loading cached tuned model: SVM RBF

Loading cached tuned model: Gaussian Naive Bayes

Loading cached tuned model: MLP Neural Network
tuned_results_df = pd.DataFrame(tuned_results).T.sort_values(
    "Best CV Macro F1",
    ascending=False
)

print("\nHyperparameter tuning summary:")

Hyperparameter tuning summary:
print(tuned_results_df)

Gaussian Naive Bayes                    0.809883   
Extra Trees                             0.791934

Gaussian Naive Bayes             {'feature_selection__max_features': 30, 'model...  
Extra Trees                      {'feature_selection__max_features': 10, 'model...

SVM RBF                          {'feature_selection__max_features': 20, 'model...  
MLP Neural Network               {'feature_selection__max_features': 5, 'model_...  
XGBoost                          {'feature_selection__max_features': 5, 'model_...

Final Grouped Cross-Validated Model Comparison

# ============================================================
# FINAL GROUPED CV MODEL COMPARISON
# ============================================================

outer_cv = GroupKFold(n_splits=5)

final_results_cache = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, f"{cache_key}_final_results.joblib")

if os.path.exists(final_results_cache):

    print("Loading cached final CV results.")
    final_results_df = joblib.load(final_results_cache)

else:

    final_results = {}

    for name, estimator in best_estimators.items():

        print("\nEvaluating tuned model:", name)

        scores = cross_validate(
            estimator,
            X,
            y,
            groups=groups,
            cv=outer_cv,
            scoring={
                "balanced_accuracy": "balanced_accuracy",
                "macro_f1": "f1_macro",
                "auroc": "roc_auc",
                "precision_macro": "precision_macro",
                "recall_macro": "recall_macro"
            },
            n_jobs=1,
            error_score="raise"
        )

        final_results[name] = {
            "Balanced Accuracy Mean": scores["test_balanced_accuracy"].mean(),
            "Balanced Accuracy SD": scores["test_balanced_accuracy"].std(),
            "Macro F1 Mean": scores["test_macro_f1"].mean(),
            "Macro F1 SD": scores["test_macro_f1"].std(),
            "AUROC Mean": scores["test_auroc"].mean(),
            "AUROC SD": scores["test_auroc"].std(),
            "Precision Macro Mean": scores["test_precision_macro"].mean(),
            "Precision Macro SD": scores["test_precision_macro"].std(),
            "Recall Macro Mean": scores["test_recall_macro"].mean(),
            "Recall Macro SD": scores["test_recall_macro"].std()
        }

    final_results_df = pd.DataFrame(final_results).T.sort_values(
        "Macro F1 Mean",
        ascending=False
    )

    joblib.dump(final_results_df, final_results_cache)
Loading cached final CV results.
print("\nFinal grouped-CV model comparison:")

Model Comparison Plots

# ============================================================
# FOUR SEPARATE MODEL PERFORMANCE BOXPLOTS
# ============================================================

fold_rows = []

for name, estimator in best_estimators.items():

    scores = cross_validate(
        estimator,
        X,
        y,
        groups=groups,
        cv=outer_cv,
        scoring={
            "Macro F1": "f1_macro",
            "AUROC": "roc_auc",
            "Balanced Accuracy": "balanced_accuracy",
            "Macro Recall": "recall_macro"
        },
        n_jobs=1,
        error_score="raise"
    )

    for i in range(5):
        fold_rows.append({
            "Model": name,
            "Macro F1": scores["test_Macro F1"][i],
            "AUROC": scores["test_AUROC"][i],
            "Balanced Accuracy": scores["test_Balanced Accuracy"][i],
            "Macro Recall": scores["test_Macro Recall"][i]
        })

fold_df = pd.DataFrame(fold_rows)

rename_map = {
    "Elastic Net Logistic Regression": "Elastic Net",
    "MLP Neural Network": "MLP",
    "Extra Trees": "Extra Trees",
    "XGBoost": "XGBoost",
    "SVM RBF": "SVM",
    "Gaussian Naive Bayes": "Gaussian NB"
}

fold_df["Model"] = fold_df["Model"].map(rename_map)

model_order = [
    "Elastic Net",
    "MLP",
    "Extra Trees",
    "XGBoost",
    "SVM",
    "Gaussian NB"
]

palette = sns.color_palette("Set2", n_colors=len(model_order))
palette_dict = dict(zip(model_order, palette))

metrics = [
    "Macro F1",
    "AUROC",
    "Balanced Accuracy",
    "Macro Recall"
]

for metric in metrics:

    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6.5))

    sns.boxplot(
        data=fold_df,
        x="Model",
        y=metric,
        order=model_order,
        palette=palette_dict,
        width=0.6,
        linewidth=1.2
    )

    sns.stripplot(
        data=fold_df,
        x="Model",
        y=metric,
        order=model_order,
        color="black",
        size=5.5,
        alpha=0.55,
        jitter=0.12
    )

    plt.title(f"Grouped Cross-Validation {metric}", fontsize=17, fontweight="bold")
    plt.xlabel("")
    plt.ylabel("Score", fontsize=13)
    plt.ylim(0.0, 1.05)

    plt.xticks(rotation=20, ha="right", fontsize=11)
    plt.yticks(fontsize=11)

    plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.35)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

Select Best Model

# ============================================================
# SELECT BEST MODEL
# ============================================================

best_model_name = final_results_df.index[0]
best_pipe = best_estimators[best_model_name]

print("Best final model:", best_model_name)
Best final model: Gaussian Naive Bayes
print("\nBest tuned parameters:")

Best tuned parameters:
print(tuned_results_df.loc[best_model_name, "Best Params"])
{'feature_selection__max_features': 30, 'model__var_smoothing': 1e-09}

Cross-Validated Predictions for Best Model

# ============================================================
# CACHED CROSS-VALIDATED PREDICTIONS FOR BEST MODEL
# ============================================================

safe_best_name = best_model_name.replace(" ", "_").replace("/", "_")
pred_cache_path = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, f"{cache_key}_{safe_best_name}_predictions.joblib")

if os.path.exists(pred_cache_path):

    print("Loading cached cross-validated predictions.")

    pred_cache = joblib.load(pred_cache_path)

    y_pred_cv = pred_cache["y_pred_cv"]
    y_proba_cv = pred_cache["y_proba_cv"]

else:

    print("Generating cross-validated predictions for best model.")

    y_pred_cv = cross_val_predict(
        best_pipe,
        X,
        y,
        groups=groups,
        cv=outer_cv,
        n_jobs=1,
        method="predict"
    )

    y_proba_cv = cross_val_predict(
        best_pipe,
        X,
        y,
        groups=groups,
        cv=outer_cv,
        n_jobs=1,
        method="predict_proba"
    )

    joblib.dump({
        "y_pred_cv": y_pred_cv,
        "y_proba_cv": y_proba_cv
    }, pred_cache_path)
Loading cached cross-validated predictions.
# ============================================================
# MODEL 1 THRESHOLD TUNING
# ============================================================

# severe = 1
positive_class = label_encoder.transform(["severe"])[0]
positive_index = list(best_pipe.classes_).index(positive_class)

severity_probs = y_proba_cv[:, positive_index]

threshold_grid = np.arange(0.05, 0.96, 0.01)

threshold_rows = []

for threshold in threshold_grid:

    pred_labels = (severity_probs >= threshold).astype(int)

    threshold_rows.append({
        "threshold": threshold,
        "macro_f1": f1_score(y, pred_labels, average="macro"),
        "balanced_accuracy": balanced_accuracy_score(y, pred_labels),
        "severe_precision": precision_score(y, pred_labels, zero_division=0),
        "severe_recall": recall_score(y, pred_labels, zero_division=0),
        "non_severe_recall": recall_score(1 - y, 1 - pred_labels, zero_division=0)
    })

threshold_df = pd.DataFrame(threshold_rows)

best_threshold_row = threshold_df.sort_values("macro_f1", ascending=False).iloc[0]
best_threshold = best_threshold_row["threshold"]

print("Best threshold based on Macro F1:")
Best threshold based on Macro F1:
print(best_threshold_row)
threshold            0.500000
macro_f1             0.819444
balanced_accuracy    0.801429
severe_precision     0.827586
severe_recall        0.960000
non_severe_recall    0.642857
Name: 45, dtype: float64
threshold_pred_labels = (severity_probs >= best_threshold).astype(int)

print("\nClassification report using tuned threshold:")

Classification report using tuned threshold:
print(classification_report(
    y,
    threshold_pred_labels,
    target_names=label_encoder.classes_
))
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

  non_severe       0.90      0.64      0.75        14
      severe       0.83      0.96      0.89        25

    accuracy                           0.85        39
   macro avg       0.86      0.80      0.82        39
weighted avg       0.85      0.85      0.84        39
ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_predictions(
    y,
    threshold_pred_labels,
    display_labels=label_encoder.classes_,
    cmap="Blues"
)
<sklearn.metrics._plot.confusion_matrix.ConfusionMatrixDisplay object at 0x117531490>
plt.title(f"Tuned Threshold Confusion Matrix - {best_model_name} - Threshold {best_threshold:.2f}")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
threshold_df.to_csv("model1_threshold_sensitivity.csv", index=False)
print("Saved model1_threshold_sensitivity.csv")
Saved model1_threshold_sensitivity.csv

Best Model Classification Report

# ============================================================
# BEST MODEL CLASSIFICATION REPORT
# ============================================================

print("Classification report for best model:")
Classification report for best model:
print(classification_report(
    y,
    y_pred_cv,
    target_names=label_encoder.classes_
))
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

  non_severe       0.90      0.64      0.75        14
      severe       0.83      0.96      0.89        25

    accuracy                           0.85        39
   macro avg       0.86      0.80      0.82        39
weighted avg       0.85      0.85      0.84        39
best_macro_f1 = f1_score(y, y_pred_cv, average="macro")
best_balanced_accuracy = balanced_accuracy_score(y, y_pred_cv)
best_precision_macro = precision_score(y, y_pred_cv, average="macro", zero_division=0)
best_recall_macro = recall_score(y, y_pred_cv, average="macro", zero_division=0)

best_auroc = roc_auc_score(y, y_proba_cv[:, 1])
best_avg_precision = average_precision_score(y, y_proba_cv[:, 1])

print("Grouped-CV Macro F1:", round(best_macro_f1, 3))
Grouped-CV Macro F1: 0.819
print("Grouped-CV Balanced Accuracy:", round(best_balanced_accuracy, 3))
Grouped-CV Balanced Accuracy: 0.801
print("Grouped-CV Macro Precision:", round(best_precision_macro, 3))
Grouped-CV Macro Precision: 0.864
print("Grouped-CV Macro Recall:", round(best_recall_macro, 3))
Grouped-CV Macro Recall: 0.801
print("Grouped-CV AUROC:", round(best_auroc, 3))
Grouped-CV AUROC: 0.783
print("Grouped-CV Average Precision:", round(best_avg_precision, 3))
Grouped-CV Average Precision: 0.805

Confusion Matrix

# ============================================================
# CONFUSION MATRIX
# ============================================================

ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_predictions(
    y,
    y_pred_cv,
    display_labels=label_encoder.classes_,
    cmap="Blues"
)
<sklearn.metrics._plot.confusion_matrix.ConfusionMatrixDisplay object at 0x115520440>
plt.title(f"Grouped-CV Confusion Matrix - {best_model_name}")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Probability Confidence Plot

# ============================================================
# PREDICTED CONFIDENCE DISTRIBUTION
# ============================================================

confidence_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Predicted confidence": np.max(y_proba_cv, axis=1),
    "True class": label_encoder.inverse_transform(y),
    "Predicted class": label_encoder.inverse_transform(y_pred_cv)
})
confidence_df["Correct"] = confidence_df["True class"] == confidence_df["Predicted class"]

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.kdeplot(
    data=confidence_df,
    x="Predicted confidence",
    hue="Correct",
    fill=True,
    common_norm=False,
    alpha=0.4
)

plt.title("Predicted Confidence Distribution by Correctness")
plt.xlabel("Maximum predicted class probability")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Feature Importance

# ============================================================
# FEATURE IMPORTANCE
# ============================================================

best_pipe.fit(X, y)
Pipeline(steps=[('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
                ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
                ('feature_selection',
                 SelectFromModel(estimator=ExtraTreesClassifier(class_weight='balanced',
                                                                min_samples_leaf=2,
                                                                n_estimators=250,
                                                                n_jobs=1,
                                                                random_state=42),
                                 max_features=30)),
                ('model', GaussianNB())])
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
steps steps: list of tuples

List of (name of step, estimator) tuples that are to be chained in
sequential order. To be compatible with the scikit-learn API, all steps
must define `fit`. All non-last steps must also define `transform`. See
:ref:`Combining Estimators ` for more details.		 [('imputer', ...), ('scaler', ...), ...]
transform_input transform_input: list of str, default=None

The names of the :term:`metadata` parameters that should be transformed by the
pipeline before passing it to the step consuming it.

This enables transforming some input arguments to ``fit`` (other than ``X``)
to be transformed by the steps of the pipeline up to the step which requires
them. Requirement is defined via :ref:`metadata routing `.
For instance, this can be used to pass a validation set through the pipeline.

You can only set this if metadata routing is enabled, which you
can enable using ``sklearn.set_config(enable_metadata_routing=True)``.

.. versionadded:: 1.6		 None
memory memory: str or object with the joblib.Memory interface, default=None

Used to cache the fitted transformers of the pipeline. The last step
will never be cached, even if it is a transformer. By default, no
caching is performed. If a string is given, it is the path to the
caching directory. Enabling caching triggers a clone of the transformers
before fitting. Therefore, the transformer instance given to the
pipeline cannot be inspected directly. Use the attribute ``named_steps``
or ``steps`` to inspect estimators within the pipeline. Caching the
transformers is advantageous when fitting is time consuming. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_neighbors_plot_caching_nearest_neighbors.py`
for an example on how to enable caching.		 None
verbose verbose: bool, default=False

If True, the time elapsed while fitting each step will be printed as it
is completed.		 False
Parameters
missing_values missing_values: int, float, str, np.nan, None or pandas.NA, default=np.nan

The placeholder for the missing values. All occurrences of
`missing_values` will be imputed. For pandas' dataframes with
nullable integer dtypes with missing values, `missing_values`
can be set to either `np.nan` or `pd.NA`.		 nan
strategy strategy: str or Callable, default='mean'

The imputation strategy.

- If "mean", then replace missing values using the mean along
each column. Can only be used with numeric data.
- If "median", then replace missing values using the median along
each column. Can only be used with numeric data.
- If "most_frequent", then replace missing using the most frequent
value along each column. Can be used with strings or numeric data.
If there is more than one such value, only the smallest is returned.
- If "constant", then replace missing values with fill_value. Can be
used with strings or numeric data.
- If an instance of Callable, then replace missing values using the
scalar statistic returned by running the callable over a dense 1d
array containing non-missing values of each column.

.. versionadded:: 0.20
strategy="constant" for fixed value imputation.

.. versionadded:: 1.5
strategy=callable for custom value imputation.		 'median'
fill_value fill_value: str or numerical value, default=None

When strategy == "constant", `fill_value` is used to replace all
occurrences of missing_values. For string or object data types,
`fill_value` must be a string.
If `None`, `fill_value` will be 0 when imputing numerical
data and "missing_value" for strings or object data types.		 None
copy copy: bool, default=True

If True, a copy of X will be created. If False, imputation will
be done in-place whenever possible. Note that, in the following cases,
a new copy will always be made, even if `copy=False`:

- If `X` is not an array of floating values;
- If `X` is encoded as a CSR matrix;
- If `add_indicator=True`.		 True
add_indicator add_indicator: bool, default=False

If True, a :class:`MissingIndicator` transform will stack onto output
of the imputer's transform. This allows a predictive estimator
to account for missingness despite imputation. If a feature has no
missing values at fit/train time, the feature won't appear on
the missing indicator even if there are missing values at
transform/test time.		 False
keep_empty_features keep_empty_features: bool, default=False

If True, features that consist exclusively of missing values when
`fit` is called are returned in results when `transform` is called.
The imputed value is always `0` except when `strategy="constant"`
in which case `fill_value` will be used instead.

.. versionadded:: 1.2		 False
Parameters
copy copy: bool, default=True

If False, try to avoid a copy and do inplace scaling instead.
This is not guaranteed to always work inplace; e.g. if the data is
not a NumPy array or scipy.sparse CSR matrix, a copy may still be
returned.		 True
with_mean with_mean: bool, default=True

If True, center the data before scaling.
This does not work (and will raise an exception) when attempted on
sparse matrices, because centering them entails building a dense
matrix which in common use cases is likely to be too large to fit in
memory.		 True
with_std with_std: bool, default=True

If True, scale the data to unit variance (or equivalently,
unit standard deviation).		 True
Parameters
estimator estimator: object

The base estimator from which the transformer is built.
This can be both a fitted (if ``prefit`` is set to True)
or a non-fitted estimator. The estimator should have a
``feature_importances_`` or ``coef_`` attribute after fitting.
Otherwise, the ``importance_getter`` parameter should be used.		 ExtraTreesCla...ndom_state=42)
threshold threshold: str or float, default=None

The threshold value to use for feature selection. Features whose
absolute importance value is greater or equal are kept while the others
are discarded. If "median" (resp. "mean"), then the ``threshold`` value
is the median (resp. the mean) of the feature importances. A scaling
factor (e.g., "1.25*mean") may also be used. If None and if the
estimator has a parameter penalty set to l1, either explicitly
or implicitly (e.g, Lasso), the threshold used is 1e-5.
Otherwise, "mean" is used by default.		 None
prefit prefit: bool, default=False

Whether a prefit model is expected to be passed into the constructor
directly or not.
If `True`, `estimator` must be a fitted estimator.
If `False`, `estimator` is fitted and updated by calling
`fit` and `partial_fit`, respectively.		 False
norm_order norm_order: non-zero int, inf, -inf, default=1

Order of the norm used to filter the vectors of coefficients below
``threshold`` in the case where the ``coef_`` attribute of the
estimator is of dimension 2.		 1
max_features max_features: int, callable, default=None

The maximum number of features to select.

- If an integer, then it specifies the maximum number of features to
allow.
- If a callable, then it specifies how to calculate the maximum number of
features allowed. The callable will receive `X` as input: `max_features(X)`.
- If `None`, then all features are kept.

To only select based on ``max_features``, set ``threshold=-np.inf``.

.. versionadded:: 0.20
.. versionchanged:: 1.1
`max_features` accepts a callable.		 30
importance_getter importance_getter: str or callable, default='auto'

If 'auto', uses the feature importance either through a ``coef_``
attribute or ``feature_importances_`` attribute of estimator.

Also accepts a string that specifies an attribute name/path
for extracting feature importance (implemented with `attrgetter`).
For example, give `regressor_.coef_` in case of
:class:`~sklearn.compose.TransformedTargetRegressor` or
`named_steps.clf.feature_importances_` in case of
:class:`~sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline` with its last step named `clf`.

If `callable`, overrides the default feature importance getter.
The callable is passed with the fitted estimator and it should
return importance for each feature.

.. versionadded:: 0.24		 'auto' 
ExtraTreesClassifier(class_weight='balanced', min_samples_leaf=2,
                     n_estimators=250, n_jobs=1, random_state=42)
Parameters
n_estimators n_estimators: int, default=100

The number of trees in the forest.

.. versionchanged:: 0.22
The default value of ``n_estimators`` changed from 10 to 100
in 0.22.		 250
criterion criterion: {"gini", "entropy", "log_loss"}, default="gini"

The function to measure the quality of a split. Supported criteria are
"gini" for the Gini impurity and "log_loss" and "entropy" both for the
Shannon information gain, see :ref:`tree_mathematical_formulation`.
Note: This parameter is tree-specific.		 'gini'
max_depth max_depth: int, default=None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than
min_samples_split samples.		 None
min_samples_split min_samples_split: int or float, default=2

The minimum number of samples required to split an internal node:

- If int, then consider `min_samples_split` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_split` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_split * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each split.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions.		 2
min_samples_leaf min_samples_leaf: int or float, default=1

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.
A split point at any depth will only be considered if it leaves at
least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

.. versionchanged:: 0.18
Added float values for fractions.		 2
min_weight_fraction_leaf min_weight_fraction_leaf: float, default=0.0

The minimum weighted fraction of the sum total of weights (of all
the input samples) required to be at a leaf node. Samples have
equal weight when sample_weight is not provided.		 0.0
max_features max_features: {"sqrt", "log2", None}, int or float, default="sqrt"

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at each
split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. versionchanged:: 1.1
The default of `max_features` changed from `"auto"` to `"sqrt"`.

Note: the search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features.		 'sqrt'
max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Grow trees with ``max_leaf_nodes`` in best-first fashion.
Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes.		 None
min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19		 0.0
bootstrap bootstrap: bool, default=False

Whether bootstrap samples are used when building trees. If False, the
whole dataset is used to build each tree.		 False
oob_score oob_score: bool or callable, default=False

Whether to use out-of-bag samples to estimate the generalization score.
By default, :func:`~sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score` is used.
Provide a callable with signature `metric(y_true, y_pred)` to use a
custom metric. Only available if `bootstrap=True`.

For an illustration of out-of-bag (OOB) error estimation, see the example
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_ensemble_plot_ensemble_oob.py`.		 False
n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of jobs to run in parallel. :meth:`fit`, :meth:`predict`,
:meth:`decision_path` and :meth:`apply` are all parallelized over the
trees. ``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend`
context. ``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary
` for more details.		 1
random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls 3 sources of randomness:

- the bootstrapping of the samples used when building trees
(if ``bootstrap=True``)
- the sampling of the features to consider when looking for the best
split at each node (if ``max_features < n_features``)
- the draw of the splits for each of the `max_features`

See :term:`Glossary ` for details.		 42
verbose verbose: int, default=0

Controls the verbosity when fitting and predicting.		 0
warm_start warm_start: bool, default=False

When set to ``True``, reuse the solution of the previous call to fit
and add more estimators to the ensemble, otherwise, just fit a whole
new forest. See :term:`Glossary ` and
:ref:`tree_ensemble_warm_start` for details.		 False
class_weight class_weight: {"balanced", "balanced_subsample"}, dict or list of dicts, default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If not given, all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems, a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example,
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 5}, {0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 1}] instead of
[{1:1}, {2:5}, {3:1}, {4:1}].

The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

The "balanced_subsample" mode is the same as "balanced" except that
weights are computed based on the bootstrap sample for every tree
grown.

For multi-output, the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified.		 'balanced'
ccp_alpha ccp_alpha: non-negative float, default=0.0

Complexity parameter used for Minimal Cost-Complexity Pruning. The
subtree with the largest cost complexity that is smaller than
``ccp_alpha`` will be chosen. By default, no pruning is performed. See
:ref:`minimal_cost_complexity_pruning` for details. See
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_tree_plot_cost_complexity_pruning.py`
for an example of such pruning.

.. versionadded:: 0.22		 0.0
max_samples max_samples: int or float, default=None

If bootstrap is True, the number of samples to draw from X
to train each base estimator.

- If None (default), then draw `X.shape[0]` samples.
- If int, then draw `max_samples` samples.
- If float, then draw `max_samples * X.shape[0]` samples. Thus,
`max_samples` should be in the interval `(0.0, 1.0]`.

.. versionadded:: 0.22		 None
monotonic_cst monotonic_cst: array-like of int of shape (n_features), default=None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonically increasing
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonically decreasing

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multiclass classifications (i.e. when `n_classes > 2`),
- multioutput classifications (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- classifications trained on data with missing values.

The constraints hold over the probability of the positive class.

Read more in the :ref:`User Guide `.

.. versionadded:: 1.4		 None
Parameters
priors priors: array-like of shape (n_classes,), default=None

Prior probabilities of the classes. If specified, the priors are not
adjusted according to the data.		 None
var_smoothing var_smoothing: float, default=1e-9

Portion of the largest variance of all features that is added to
variances for calculation stability.

.. versionadded:: 0.20		 1e-09 
selector = best_pipe.named_steps["feature_selection"]
model = best_pipe.named_steps["model"]

selected_features = X.columns[selector.get_support()]

if hasattr(model, "feature_importances_"):
    importance_values = model.feature_importances_

elif hasattr(model, "coef_"):
    importance_values = np.mean(np.abs(model.coef_), axis=0)

else:
    importance_values = np.repeat(np.nan, len(selected_features))

importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": selected_features,
    "importance": importance_values
}).sort_values("importance", ascending=False)

print("Top selected predictors:")
Top selected predictors:
print(importance_df.head(30))
                     feature  importance
0   Sudden onset of symptoms         NaN
1            Chest tightness         NaN
2                    Myalgia         NaN
3                   Polypnea         NaN
4                   Cyanosis         NaN
5     Chronic kidney failure         NaN
6                    Smoking         NaN
7                  Guanosine         NaN
8                    Inosine         NaN
9                    Taurine         NaN
10                        C0         NaN
11               PC aa C34:2         NaN
12               PC aa C36:1         NaN
13               PC aa C40:5         NaN
14           Cer(d18:1/22:0)         NaN
15             DG(16:0_16:0)         NaN
16             DG(16:0_18:1)         NaN
17             DG(16:0_18:2)         NaN
18             DG(18:1_18:1)         NaN
19             DG(18:1_18:2)         NaN
20             TG(16:0_38:4)         NaN
21             TG(16:0_38:6)         NaN
22             TG(20:5_36:2)         NaN
23     2-Hydroxybutyric acid         NaN
24  2-Hydroxyisovaleric acid         NaN
25        N-Acetyl-Histidine         NaN
26                     C12:1         NaN
27                  CE(16:1)         NaN
28             DG(14:0_18:2)         NaN
29           Cer(d18:2/22:0)         NaN
top_n = 15

plot_importance = importance_df.head(top_n).copy()

plot_importance["short_feature"] = [
    f[:38] + "..." if len(f) > 38 else f
    for f in plot_importance["feature"]
]

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))

plt.barh(
    plot_importance["short_feature"][::-1],
    plot_importance["importance"][::-1]
)

plt.xlabel("Importance")
plt.ylabel("")
plt.title(f"Top {top_n} Selected Predictors - {best_model_name}")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Save Report Tables

# ============================================================
# SAVE REPORT TABLES
# ============================================================

final_results_df.to_csv("model1_binary_severity_grouped_cv_results.csv")
tuned_results_df.to_csv("model1_binary_severity_tuned_hyperparameters.csv")
importance_df.to_csv("model1_binary_severity_feature_importance.csv", index=False)

print("Saved report tables:")
Saved report tables:
print("- model1_binary_severity_grouped_cv_results.csv")
- model1_binary_severity_grouped_cv_results.csv
print("- model1_binary_severity_tuned_hyperparameters.csv")
- model1_binary_severity_tuned_hyperparameters.csv
print("- model1_binary_severity_feature_importance.csv")
- model1_binary_severity_feature_importance.csv

Final Summary

# ============================================================
# FINAL SUMMARY
# ============================================================

print("================ FINAL SUMMARY ================")
================ FINAL SUMMARY ================
print("Aim: Predict binary admission severity class from clinical and metabolomic predictors")
Aim: Predict binary admission severity class from clinical and metabolomic predictors
print("Target: non_severe = moderate; severe = severe + critical")
Target: non_severe = moderate; severe = severe + critical
print("Leakage control: GroupKFold by Sample ID / patient group")
Leakage control: GroupKFold by Sample ID / patient group
print("Samples:", X.shape[0])
Samples: 39
print("Unique groups:", groups.nunique())
Unique groups: 39
print("Predictors before selection:", X.shape[1])
Predictors before selection: 568
print("Best model:", best_model_name)
Best model: Gaussian Naive Bayes
print("Grouped-CV Macro F1:", round(best_macro_f1, 3))
Grouped-CV Macro F1: 0.819
print("Grouped-CV Balanced Accuracy:", round(best_balanced_accuracy, 3))
Grouped-CV Balanced Accuracy: 0.801
print("Grouped-CV AUROC:", round(best_auroc, 3))
Grouped-CV AUROC: 0.783
print("Grouped-CV Average Precision:", round(best_avg_precision, 3))
Grouped-CV Average Precision: 0.805
print("\nBest model grouped CV performance:")

Best model grouped CV performance:
print(final_results_df.loc[[best_model_name]].to_string())
                      Balanced Accuracy Mean  Balanced Accuracy SD  Macro F1 Mean  Macro F1 SD  AUROC Mean  AUROC SD  Precision Macro Mean  Precision Macro SD  Recall Macro Mean  Recall Macro SD
Gaussian Naive Bayes                0.796667              0.101871       0.809883     0.098563    0.771667  0.140594              0.882381            0.091394           0.796667         0.101871
print("================================================")
================================================

Interpretation Notes

This Model 1 workflow uses the same machine-learning structure as Model 2, including grouped cross-validation, embedded tree-based feature selection, six candidate model families, and Macro F1-score for hyperparameter optimisation. The main difference is that the target is now binary admission severity classification rather than multiclass severity prediction.

The binary target combines severe and critical patients into a single severe class, while moderate patients are treated as non_severe. Model performance is interpreted using Macro F1-score, balanced accuracy, AUROC, macro precision, macro recall, and the confusion matrix.

# ============================================================
# FULL FINAL RESULTS TABLE
# ============================================================

pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
pd.set_option("display.width", 200)

print(final_results_df.round(3))

# ============================================================
# SAVE MODEL 1 DEPLOYMENT FEATURE PANEL
# ============================================================

deployment_features = selected_features.tolist()

deployment_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": deployment_features
})

deployment_df.to_csv(
    "model1_deployment_features.csv",
    index=False
)

print("\nSaved deployment feature panel:")

Saved deployment feature panel:

model1_deployment_features.csv
